# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kong configuration for API routes that proxies to microservices. Configure upstream services, routes, and plugins for a production API gateway.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for api gateway security and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: api_gateway_security

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata: 
 name: kong-service   # service_name should be unique within a namespace, and it must match with your deployment's selector key in Deployment or Ingress configuration (e.g., app=kubernetes) if you are using K8S ingress controller for routing traffic to services/routes
spec: 
 type: LoadBalancer   # This will expose the service as a load balancing target, which is useful when your microservices need external accessibility and can be accessed from outside. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
 selector:  # This will match the labels of pods that should receive incoming requests, which is useful when your microservices need a consistent identity for their clients and can be accessed from outside. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
   app: kong-service  # This should match the 'app' label of your pods, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
 ports:  # This will define the port(s) that should be exposed by your service, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
   - port: 80 # This should match the 'port' label of your pod, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
     targetPort: 80 # This should match the 'target_port' label of your pod, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
     protocol: TCP # This should match the 'protocol' label of your pod, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
     name: http # This should match the 'name' label of your pod, which is useful when Kubernetes manages Pods and ReplicaSets for a Service. If not specified (i.e., if you are using Ingress), then only internal traffic to services/routes within this cluster would get routed
---  # This marks the end of kong-service definition, which is required by Kubernetes YAML formatting rules and should be placed at least once per file for a valid configuration. It's not necessary if you are using Ingress controller or similar controllers that manage traffic routing to services/routes
